candidate_problems.text: How can one rigorously distinguish a true lock-in equilibrium from persistence driven mainly by ideology, misinformation, habit, or direct elite coordination? The conjecture hinges on this separation, but in real systems these mechanisms are entangled, so a major open problem is identifying observable signatures, tests, or models that discriminate structural equilibrium reproduction from other causes of persistence.

What transition mechanisms can actually move a power system from a domination-preserving equilibrium to a fairer equilibrium without triggering collapse, backlash, or rapid re-lock-in? The conjecture says reform must change incentives, coordination, and first-mover risk, but it leaves open which interventions are sufficient, under what conditions, and how sequencing, scale, and institutional design affect success.

How endogenous is 'legibility capture'—that is, by what mechanisms do power structures come to define merit, competence, and realism in ways that stabilize themselves, and when can alternative standards become self-sustaining? This is an important unresolved tension because the conjecture treats legibility as a stabilizing mechanism, but it remains unclear whether it is merely downstream of material incentives or an independent causal layer with its own dynamics and leverage points.
